question_text: A student heats 100 grams of water in a beaker. The water reaches its boiling point of 100°C. The student continues heating for another 5 minutes, but the temperature does NOT increase beyond 100°C. Explain why the temperature stays at 100°C even though heat energy is still being added.
correct_answer: The heat energy being added is used to break the bonds between particles and change the water from liquid to gas (evaporation/boiling), rather than increasing the temperature. This energy is called latent heat. The temperature only increases once all the water has boiled away.
explanation: At the boiling point, any heat energy supplied goes into changing the state of matter (latent heat of vaporisation), not into increasing kinetic energy and temperature. This is a key concept: during a change of state, temperature remains constant even when heat is added. Once the change of state is complete, further heating will increase the temperature again.
